A working guide to BAAs, zero retention, and the difference between “we support HIPAA” and “your PHI is safe”

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every week, a version of the same conversation happens inside a healthcare organization.
A clinician discovers that pasting a discharge summary into an AI assistant turns forty minutes of documentation into four. Word spreads. Within a month, a dozen people are doing it. Then someone in compliance asks the obvious question — is this allowed? — and the answer arrives too late to matter.
The uncomfortable part isn’t that people are careless. It’s that the tools are genuinely useful, the vendor marketing genuinely says “HIPAA,” and almost nobody reads far enough to find the line where coverage stops.
So let’s read that far.

The core misunderstanding: HIPAA compliance is not a product feature
HIPAA compliance is not something a model possesses. It’s a property of a relationship — specifically, a legal one between a covered entity and a business associate, formalized in a Business Associate Agreement (BAA).
No BAA, no compliant use of protected health information. It doesn’t matter how good the encryption is. It doesn’t matter that the vendor is SOC 2 certified. It doesn’t matter that you removed the patient’s name.
This produces three distinct failure modes, and they’re worth naming separately because organizations conflate them constantly:
1. No BAA at all. The consumer and prosumer tiers of nearly every major assistant. Using them with PHI is a violation on the first message, not the hundredth.
2. A BAA that exists but excludes the surface you’re using. This is the sneaky one. An organization signs a BAA covering the API, and then engineers prototype in the web console — which the same agreement explicitly carves out. The paperwork is real. The coverage isn’t. Anthropic’s Claude Console is a textbook example, and the exclusion is specific enough that it’s worth reading in full.
3. A BAA that covers you legally but leaves data in a commercial environment. Technically compliant, and still not what most compliance officers picture when they approve it.
That third category is where the interesting arguments live, so we’ll come back to it.

Where the major assistants actually stand
Vendor positions shift, so treat this as a snapshot to verify rather than gospel — but the shape of it has been stable for a while.
Platform BAA available? The catch ChatGPT (Free / Plus / Pro) No Consumer tiers are outside any BAA. PHI here is a violation. ChatGPT Enterprise / API Yes, on request Requires enterprise procurement; not available on self-serve tiers Claude (Free / Pro / Max / Team) No Same consumer exclusion pattern Claude Enterprise / API Yes Console explicitly excluded; Primary Owner must enable and accept Google Gemini Varies by Workspace tier Coverage depends heavily on which surface and which admin settings Microsoft Copilot Yes, under Microsoft’s BAA Scope varies by product; consumer Copilot is not covered
Two full breakdowns worth bookmarking if you’re building a policy: what OpenAI’s BAA actually covers and the equivalent analysis for Anthropic. Both are maintained against current vendor documentation rather than written once and abandoned — which matters more than it should, because these terms move.
For teams already inside Google Workspace, the Gemini privacy picture has its own complications that don’t reduce cleanly to a yes or no.

The question nobody asks: compliant where?
Here’s the gap that separates a compliance checkbox from actual data safety.
A signed BAA tells you a vendor has accepted legal responsibility for PHI. It tells you almost nothing about the physical and logical environment your data sits in.
Most enterprise AI runs on commercial cloud infrastructure — the same regions and tenancy models serving retail, adtech, and social platforms. That’s not inherently reckless. It’s just a different security posture than the one federal health agencies operate under.
The stricter standard is FedRAMP High, and in practice that means environments like AWS GovCloud: isolated regions, personnel screening requirements, and control baselines drawn from NIST 800–53 and NIST 800–171. It’s the boundary the Department of Health and Human Services works inside.
Very few commercially available AI tools operate there. Hathr.AI is one of them — running Anthropic’s Claude models inside AWS GovCloud with a BAA attached to every account rather than reserved for enterprise contracts.
The distinction is worth internalizing even if you never buy anything: “HIPAA compliant” and “private” are different claims. A vendor can be fully compliant and still retain your prompts for abuse monitoring, model evaluation, or human review. Zero data retention is a separate commitment, and you should ask for it explicitly. Hathr’s approach to that separation is a reasonable reference point for what to demand from any vendor.

Why “we de-identified it” usually doesn’t hold
The most common workaround is also the weakest.
The HIPAA Safe Harbor method requires removing eighteen identifier categories. Names and MRNs are obvious. The ones teams miss:

• All date elements more precise than year — including admission, discharge, and death dates

• Ages over 89

• Geographic subdivisions smaller than a state, including ZIP codes in sparse areas

• Device identifiers and serial numbers

• Any other unique identifying number or characteristic
A clinical narrative stripped of names still routinely contains a rare diagnosis, an admission date, and a referring facility. In a small population, that combination re-identifies a patient without much effort.
De-identification is a legitimate strategy. Ad-hoc de-identification performed by a busy clinician at 7 p.m. is not, and treating it as a compliance control is how organizations end up with breach notification obligations they didn’t anticipate.

A procurement checklist that actually filters
If you’re evaluating tools, these seven questions separate serious vendors from marketing pages. Ask them in writing.

• Will you sign a BAA, and how long does it take? Days is reasonable. Months signals the compliance posture is aspirational. Hathr signs within 24 hours; most enterprise processes run considerably longer.

• Which specific surfaces does the BAA cover? Get the list. Web app, API, console, mobile, browser extension — enumerate them.

• What is the data retention policy, in days? “We don’t train on your data” is not the same as “we don’t store your data.”

• Where is data physically processed? Commercial cloud, government cloud, or on-premises. Ask for the region.

• Which compliance frameworks are certified, not merely aligned with? FedRAMP, HITRUST, SOC 2 Type II, NIST 800–171. “Aligned with” is a word that does a lot of work.

• Can employees or contractors access prompt content? Under what circumstances, and is it logged?

• What audit logging do we receive? You need this for your own compliance obligations, not just theirs.
If a vendor can’t answer all seven quickly, they haven’t thought about it as hard as you need them to. A structured comparison of the current tools against these criteria is a faster starting point than building the matrix yourself.
For teams weighing purpose-built options against each other, there are honest head-to-head breakdowns of Hathr versus BastionGPT and Hathr versus CompliantChatGPT — including where the alternatives are the better fit.

The short version
Consumer AI tiers are never covered. No BAA, no exceptions, no careful-usage workaround.
Enterprise tiers can be covered, with conditions. Read which surfaces the agreement names. The exclusions are where organizations get hurt.
Compliance and privacy are separate purchases. Ask about retention explicitly, in days.
Infrastructure is a real variable. FedRAMP High and commercial cloud are meaningfully different security postures, and you should know which one you’re buying.
Ad-hoc de-identification is not a control. Eighteen categories, and the dates are the ones people forget.
None of this argues against using AI in healthcare. The productivity gains are real and, for understaffed teams, close to transformative. The argument is narrower: do the twenty minutes of diligence before the tool touches patient data, not after.

If you’re evaluating options, Hathr.AI runs Anthropic’s Claude inside AWS GovCloud with a BAA on every account and zero data retention — pricing and plan details are here, and the API documentation is here if you’re integrating rather than using the chat interface. Questions about a specific workflow are best directed at the team.
This piece reflects publicly available vendor documentation as of publication and is not legal advice. Verify current terms with your vendors and counsel before setting policy.
